rulings.Case Title: MANUEL ELAURIA et al VS. SUPERSHUTTLE INTERNATIONAL, INC. et al
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 30, 2008, line 1. DEFENDANTS SUPERSHUTTLE INTERNATIONAL, INC AND TOM LEVOY'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT is sustained without leave to amend. The Court takes judicial notice of the arbitration award between plaintiffs and Super Shuttle San Francisco ("S S S F"). The issue there was whether S S S F breached the Uniform Franchise Agreement ("U F A") related to obtaining uninsured motorist coverage, breached a fiduciary duty to Mr. Elauria or defrauded Mr. Elauria. In deciding the fraud claim, the arbitrator addressed the contention that S S S F failed to provide reasonably necessary insurance and that S S S F allegedly profited through its insurance program. The arbitrator ruled against Mr. Elauria on both issues and also found that there was no breach of the U F A agreement. Plaintiff's complaint makes the same claims against these defendants - S S S F's parent and an alleged owner of the parent. The central claim against these defendants centers on obtaining vehicle insurance, overcharging franchises for the insurance and misappropriating the franchises' insurance payments (paragraph 11). Central to the claims made is the contention that section 5 of the U F A made false representations as to insurance coverage (paragraphs 28-32). Each of plaintiff's causes of action arises directly the issues litigated and decided in the prior arbitration. For that reason the arbitration is res judicata. =(302/PJM)

rulings.Case Title: BERNARD ROGOVER VS. A. P. GREEN INDUSTRIES, INC. (A.P. GRREN et al
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 30, 2008, line 5. DEFENDANT INTERNATIONAL PAPER COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. The motion is denied. Defendant did not shift the burden of production. Plaintiff's interrogatories identify a limited number of job sites and witnesses and there has been no showing that the witnesses cannot offer more specific testimony than plaintiff. In addition, plaintiff has presented the declarations of plaintiff, Mojica and Ay that create a triable issue of fact on the issue of exposure. =(302/PJM/AY)

rulings.Case Title: JESSIE EVANS VS. THE UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 30, 2008, line 11. DEFENDANTS THE UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO, DEBRA GORE-MANN AND MICHAEL VATAIN'S MOTION TO STRIKE is granted in part and denied in part. The anti-SLAPP statute applies to the defamation claim, counts 5 and 6. Evans is a public figure; the comments at issue relate to that position, and as to those causes of action the moving parties have established the first prong. McGarry v. Univ. of San Diego (2007) 154 Cal. App. 4th 97, 115. Plaintiff has not demonstrated an ability to prevail on these claims. The record reflects plaintiff's agreement with the "leave of absence" reference and so this is not actionable under Royer v. Steinberg (1979) 90 Cal. App. 3d 490, 498. Neither the complaint nor plaintiff's evidence sets forth what Ms. Gore-Mann stated regarding misconduct at plaintiff's prior employment. As to Mr. Vartain's statements, plaintiff has presented no evidence of malice as required under McGarry at 114. The motion is denied as to the 4th cause of action for it is alleged only against Does. The second and third causes of action center on plaintiff's hiring, employment relationship and termination. The claims embrace far more than protected conduct and for that reason, defendant's have not met the first prong of CCP 425.16. Given the ruling on the fraud claim, the motion is denied as to punitive damages. =(302/PJM/PB)

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: UNITED EDUCATORS OF SAN FRANCISCO, AFT/CFT,AFL-
rulings.Calendar Matter: (Second) Re Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, July 30, 2008, line 12. PETITIONER UNITED EDUCATORS OF SAN FRANCISCO, AFT/CFT,AFL'S Petition To Compel Arbitration is denied without prejudice to amend for failure to describe the circumstances related to each claimant that demonstrates that his or her claim is subject to arbitration and that the preliminary steps set forth in the grievance procedure, section 19, have been met. Graphic Arts Int'l Union v. Oakland Nat. Engraving Co. (1986) 185 Cal App 3d 775, 780-783. =(302/PJM/AY)
